# Generated by ollama (starcoder2)
# Prompt: Write a Deployment Manager template for a complete GCP application stack (Compute Engine, Cloud SQL, Cloud Storage, Load Balancer). Focus on core infrastructure. Skip Cloud Logging and Monitoring configuration to reduce complexity in our initial MVP deployment.
# Category: cloud_monitoring

Cloud Organization for a shared tenant that contains multiple environments. Environment may include Production (prod), Staging (stg), and Development (dev) environments. These environments will be used to isolate workloads. All environments should be isolated from each other. This allows to safely deploy workloads without impacting each other or share resources with each other.

Example: org-dev

Cloud Projects that belong to the Organization. A project contains all of your cloud resources, such as Cloud Compute Engine VMs and Docker containers. The resources in one project are isolated from those in others. Projects may contain resources for a single product (for example, an application), multiple products, or no products. In environments, each project is used to isolate workloads running on GCP.

Example: project-prod1036

Cloud Storage bucket that contains files used by Compute Engine instances. All files in a bucket are served from a single URL endpoint and share the same DNS name. The DNS name depends on your Cloud Platform region. It is of the format project-number-regional-ID.storage.googleapis.com, where ID is generated for you when you create the bucket. Regionality allows you to distribute objects using high-availability and low-latency networks.

Example: gs://project-prod1036

Compute Engine instance that runs a docker container. A container is a set of software packages designed to run together. Containers are often used by microservices architectures to package and deploy components with limited dependencies into an isolated environment. Docker also provides many other benefits such as security, portability across OS platforms (Windows), version control (in case of application upgrades) , and multi-host environments (using Google Compute Engine). 

Example: gcp-project1-docker-container-instance2

Cloud SQL instance that stores the data for a cloud database. Cloud SQL instances can be configured in different virtual machine sizes to maximize cost efficiency or CPU, memory and disk I/O resources available. These configurations will be used by Compute Engine instances during deployment. 

Example: mysql-prod1036-sql

Load Balancer that contains one or more forwarding rules. A forwarding rule allows you to specify the ports on which traffic will be sent to a backend service (compute engine instance). Cloud Load Balancing uses a combination of Google-managed load balancers and custom load balancer pools. Google managed load balancers perform automatic health checks on each backend instance and ensure that healthy backends are kept alive in the pool, so the user does not need to configure their application or backend services to enable load balancing capabilities.
